title: Macroscopic Spin GHZ States with a Levitated Ferromagnet
authors: Xueqi Ni, Zhixing Zou, Ping Koy Lam, Tao Wang, Jiangbin Gong
abstract: The generation of macroscopic quantum states can drive both fundamental physics and quantum technologies. This work proposes a top-down approach to the generation of macroscopic spin GHZ states using a levitated ferromagnet, where a strong locking between the collective spin and the lattice rotation enables mechanical control of the collective spin. We quantify the metrological advantage of the resulting macrospin superposition state by showing that Heisenberg scaling of the quantum Fisher information is achievable. Roles of symmetry and geometry are analyzed in terms of decoherence due to gas collisions, identifying accessible conditions for experimental realization. The usefulness of a macrospin superposition state of a levitated cylindrical ferromagnet in testing spin-dependent wavefunction collapse models is also discussed.
summary: This paper proposes using a levitated ferromagnet to generate macroscopic spin GHZ states by exploiting strong coupling between the collective spin and the mechanical rotor. By analyzing the constrained dynamics, the authors demonstrate that the system can achieve Heisenberg-limited precision for quantum sensing. The work is significant for both fundamental tests of quantum mechanics and developing ultra-sensitive quantum sensors.
main_problem: The work focuses on generating and characterizing macroscopic quantum states, specifically GHZ states, using a levitated ferromagnet to study quantum-to-classical transitions and test fundamental physics.
main_result: The strong spin-lattice coupling allows the mechanical degree of freedom to coherently drive the spin system into a non-classical regime, achieving near-Heisenberg-limited metrological scaling.
method: The authors employ a top-down approach, analyzing the system dynamics by constraining the Hilbert space based on the spin-lattice locking condition and calculating the Quantum Fisher Information.
model_or_system: The physical system is a levitated ferromagnet whose collective spin is coupled to its mechanical rotation (lattice). The dynamics are governed by a Hamiltonian involving spin-lattice coupling, and the evolution is analyzed using master equations and Wigner quasiprobability distributions.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), macroscopic spin superposition state signatures (GHZ state), and the spin-lattice locking correlator $\langle \hat{s} \cdot \hat{n} angle$.
important_parameters: Decoherence rates ($\Gamma_{	ext{dec}}$), required ultrahigh vacuum pressures ($\sim 10^{-9} 	ext{ Pa}$), and the spin number $N$ (up to $10^7$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies heavily on the strong spin-lattice locking condition to define a constrained subspace, and approximations are made for decoherence modeling (e.g., high-temperature limits).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spin-lattice coupling schematic, the double-well potential for GHZ state generation, the phase space representation of the macrospin GHZ states, and the scaling of QFI with spin number.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical platform and the strong coupling mechanism, to deriving the effective dynamics and constrained Hilbert space, quantifying the metrological advantage via QFI, and finally analyzing decoherence from environmental noise sources.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it merges concepts from cavity QED (trapping/levitation), quantum sensing (QFI scaling), and open quantum systems (Lindblad master equations) to realize a macroscopic quantum system.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Spin-lattice coupling in a levitated ferromag- net. (a) Schematic of a levitated ferromagnet, where the collective spin couples to the mechanical rotation of the lat- tice. The orientation of the lattice is depicted by a rotation angle ˆϕ in Cartesian coordinates. (b) Time evolution of the spin (left) and mechanical rotor (right) degrees of freedom in a static magnetic field, demonstrating locked precessional motion with finite-size dynamical fluctuations arising from spin-lattice angular-momentum exchange.

FIG. 2. Generation and phase space representation of macrospin GHZ states. (a) Illustration of a levitated fer- romagnet in an angular double-well potential. (b) Delocalization time tE versus spin number N. Numerical simulation results (dots) follow a logarithmic dependence fitted by tE ≈2.48 ln N (dashed line), using parameters (V1, V2, V3)/NℏωL = (−0.015, 0.05, −0.025). (c) Conceptual mapping of the full tensor-product Hilbert space onto the constrained subspace. (d) ⟨ˆs · ˆn⟩vs time, reflecting strong spin-lattice locking. (e) Real part of effective density matrix ˆρl eff in the rotor basis. Basis indices are ordered from 0 to 2π. (f) Real part of the effective density matrix ˆρs eff in...

FIG. 3. Readout protocol and Heisenberg-limited magnetometry. (a-b) Spin Husimi Q distributions repre- senting the recombination of superimposed states via free ro- tation, and corresponding probability distributions of magne- tization angle ϕs (a) before and (b) after recombination. (c) Quantum Fisher information for a transverse magnetic field By as a function of time (left) and number of spins (right).

FIG. 4. Gas collisions and decoherence time scales. (a) For a non-symmetric object, a single gas-molecule collision can reveal the underlying orientation and thus induce rapid decoherence. (b) Schematics of specular and diffuse collisions, as explained in the main text. (c) Required pressures and tem- peratures corresponding to different decoherence time scales due to diffuse gas collisions for a cylinder with aspect ratio R/L = 0.5 and N = 107, surrounded by N2 gas molecules. The dashed line corresponds to a 1 µs coherence window.
